og.title: Moisture, Mould & Indoor Air Quality — Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free CARSI course on the link between moisture, mould and indoor air quality — spore activation, MVOCs, high-risk materials, HVAC and humidity, health effects, air sampling, drying and remediation.

faq.q: How quickly can mould grow after water damage?
faq.a: Once moisture is introduced, dormant spores can germinate and grow into mature colonies within 24 to 48 hours, especially in warm, nutrient-rich conditions. This is why rapid drying is central to preventing mould.

faq.q: What indoor humidity level encourages mould?
faq.a: Relative humidity consistently above 60% strongly favours mould growth. Keeping indoor RH between 30% and 50% is widely recommended to limit mould and maintain occupant comfort.

faq.q: Does visible mould always require air sampling?
faq.a: No. In many cases visible mould is enough to warrant remediation. Air sampling is most useful for suspected hidden mould, real estate transactions or legal cases, and results should be interpreted by a qualified professional against outdoor comparison samples.

Restoration, building, environmental health and facilities professionals who want a stronger foundation in identifying, investigating and managing moisture and mould, and in protecting indoor air quality.
